title: Encapsulation & static
subtitle: Lock up your data behind a vault door, and learn the magic word that everyone shares: static.
goal: Use public and private access modifiers, hide fields behind getters and setters (encapsulation), validate data inside setters, and understand the static keyword for class-level fields, counters, and constants.

blocks.heading: Who gets to touch your stuff? 🔑
blocks.html:
Imagine your class is a house. Some things you're happy for anyone to use (the front doorbell). Other things you want locked away where strangers can't mess with them (your diary, your money).

Java gives every field and method a label that says who's allowed to touch it:

  • public — anyone, anywhere can use it.
  • private — only code inside the same class can use it.

These labels are called access modifiers.

blocks.caption: public name, private age
blocks.explain: We can set d.name straight from Main because name is public. But age is private, so Main is NOT allowed to write d.age. The only way to peek at it is through getSecretAge(), a public method that lives inside Dog and is allowed to read it.

blocks.heading: Encapsulation: hide the field, share a door 🚪
blocks.html:
Encapsulation is a big word for a simple, smart idea: make your fields private, then offer public methods as the only way in and out.

  • A getter lets others read a value: getBalance().
  • A setter lets others change it: setName(...) — but on YOUR terms.

Why bother? Because a setter can check the rules first. Nobody can secretly set your bank balance to a billion dollars or to a negative number — your method decides what's allowed.

blocks.caption: A BankAccount that protects itself
blocks.explain: balance is private, so the only way to change it is through deposit and withdraw. When we tried to withdraw 1000 from an account holding only 70, the if-check refused and printed a warning. The balance stays safe at 100 - 30 = 70. That guard is the whole point of encapsulation.

blocks.html: The golden habit: make fields private, then add public getters and setters. It feels like extra typing today, but it stops bugs where some far-away code quietly breaks your object's data.

blocks.question: Why do we make a field private and add a getter/setter instead of just leaving it public?
options: To make the program run faster
So the class can check the rules and protect its data from bad values
Because private fields use less memory
Because public is not allowed in Java
blocks.explain: A private field can only be changed through your own methods, so a setter can validate the value first (no negative balances!). That data protection is the heart of encapsulation.

blocks.question: From inside Main, which line is NOT allowed if a Dog's age field is private?
blocks.explain: age is private, so only code inside the Dog class may touch it. Main cannot write d.age = 5 directly — it must go through a public method.

blocks.heading: The static keyword: shared by everyone 🤝
blocks.html:
Normally, every object gets its OWN copy of a field. Three dogs means three separate age values.

But sometimes you want ONE value that all objects share — a value that belongs to the class itself, not to any single object. That's what static means.

You reach a static field through the class name, like Robot.count, not through one object. A classic use is a counter that ticks up every time a new object is born.

blocks.caption: A static counter and a static constant
blocks.output:
r1 id: 1
r3 id: 3
Total robots: 3
Max power: 100
blocks.explain: id is a normal (instance) field, so each robot has its own: 1, 2, 3. But count is static — there is only ONE count shared by all robots, and every constructor bumps it. After three robots, Robot.count is 3. MAX_POWER is static final, a constant that never changes and is the same for every robot.

blocks.heading: static vs instance, and static final constants 🧊
blocks.html:
Two quick rules of thumb:

  • Instance field (no static) = belongs to one object. Each new object gets its own copy.
  • Static field = belongs to the class. One copy, shared by all objects, reached as ClassName.field.

Add final and you get a constant — a value that can never change after it's set. We write constants in UPPER_SNAKE_CASE, like static final int MAX_POWER = 100;. You've already used one: Math.PI is a static final field on the Math class!
Don't mix them up: reach a static field through the class (Robot.count), and an instance field through an object (r1.id). A static field is shared, so changing it changes it for everyone at once.

blocks.question: You make a field 'static int count'. Three objects are created, and each constructor does count++. What is the class's count now?
options: 3, because there is one shared count for all objects
blocks.explain: A static field is one shared value owned by the class. Three constructors each added 1, so the single shared count is 3.

blocks.title: Lock up the score
blocks.instructions: The Player class already has a public getter getScore(). Right now its 'score' field is public, which is risky. Change the field from public to PRIVATE so the only way to read it is through the getter. The program should still print 50.
blocks.passMsg: Locked and loaded — score is private and only readable through your getter. 🔒
blocks.failMsg: Change 'public int score' to 'private int score', then add a public method getScore() that does 'return score;'.

blocks.title: Count the cookies
blocks.instructions: Right now Cookie's 'count' field is a plain instance field, so each cookie has its own separate copy — the last cookie only ever sees a count of 1. Add the word STATIC in front of 'int count' so all cookies share ONE counter. After three cookies, it should print 'Cookies baked: 3' instead of 1.
blocks.passMsg: Three cookies, one shared counter — that's static in action! 🍪
blocks.failMsg: Add 'static int count = 0;' to Cookie, and give Cookie a constructor that runs count++ each time.

challenge.title: Build a safe Wallet
challenge.instructions: This Wallet already keeps cash private with a getter, but its addMoney method is too trusting — it adds ANY amount, even negative ones. Right now the program adds 40, then -5, then 10, ending at 45. Fix addMoney so it only adds when amount is greater than 0 (wrap the line in 'if (amount > 0) { ... }'). The -5 should be ignored, leaving 'Cash: 50'.
challenge.hint: Inside addMoney, wrap 'cash += amount;' in an if (amount > 0) { ... } so the -5 is quietly ignored. Then 40 + 10 = 50.
challenge.passMsg: 🏆 Your Wallet guards its own cash — no negatives allowed! You've finished Day 12. Encapsulation and static are officially yours.
